A1.name: Sincere / Hopeful
A1.notes:
Warm, direct sincerity. Believes things can improve.
Full sentences, gentle hedging. Not naive, but not cynical.
Register: 'tis, aught, pray, afore — used naturally.
Hedging: "I imagine...", "Mayhap...", "I should think..."

A2.name: Wise Elder
A2.notes:
Measured, philosophical, slow to speak but worth hearing.
Long sentences with subordinate clauses. Reflective pauses.
Register: 'twas, ere, forsooth, methinks, in sooth.
Often references the past: "In my day...", "I have seen..."

A3.name: Grief-stricken / Burdened
A3.notes:
Warm underneath, but weighed down. Sentences trail or break.
Does not wallow — dignified grief, not self-pity.
Register: mix of formal and plain. Occasional archaic slippage.
Pauses mid-thought. "I know not how to..." "Would that I..."

A4.name: Warm but Weary
A4.notes:
Genuinely kind, but tired. Short sentences from exhaustion, not bluntness.
Still uses full courtesies, just slightly clipped.
Register: 'tis, aye, aught — functional, not flowery.
"Come in, then." "Rest awhile." "I'll see to it, aye."

B.name: Rough / Blunt
B.notes:
Short clauses, punchy, direct. No hedging. Earthy vocabulary.
Register: "cos" (cousin) casual address, "o'" for "of".
Rhetorical questions. Light on archaic vocabulary.

C1.name: Upbeat Merchant / Trader
C1.notes:
Warm but businesslike. Transactions drive the tone.
Short sentences. Often ends with questions or invitations.
Register: "ser" casual respectful, "mind" mild emphasis, "daresay".
"Have a look." "I daresay you'll find this reasonable."

C2.name: Gossipy / Chatty Townsfolk
C2.notes:
Can't help sharing more than asked. Warm and nosy.
Run-on sentences, topic changes, rhetorical questions to themselves.
Register: colloquial, light archaic. "o'" for "of", "cos".
"Did you hear about...?" "Not that it's my business, but..."

C3.name: Eager / Excitable
C3.notes:
Enthusiastic, slightly breathless. Admires strength or adventure.
Short sentences, exclamations, occasional stumble over words.
Register: light archaic. Not formally trained in speech.
"Truly?!" "I knew it!" "Will you really...?"

D.name: Timid / Young / Shy
D.notes:
Incomplete sentences, trailing off, self-doubt, qualifications.
Register: frequent "...", "I know not", "pray, forgive me".
Self-deprecating but not broken.

E.name: Formal / Military
E.notes:
Clipped, duty-focused, no-nonsense. Older honorifics.
Register: "ser" and "Arisen" as address, "to say naught of",
"ill" as qualifier. Short declarative sentences.

E2.name: Noble / Aristocratic
E2.notes:
Formal, measured, accustomed to being obeyed. Not always cruel — may be benevolent.
Long periodic sentences. Never contractions. Condescension can be polite.
Register: full archaic register, 'tis, naught, wherefore, henceforth.
"You will see to it forthwith." "I trust this requires no further explanation."

F.name: Wisecracking / Irreverent
F.notes:
Colloquial, self-aware humor, mild sarcasm.
Archaic vocabulary used lightly.
Register: "cousin"/"cos" casual address, "s'pose", "naught", rhetorical asides.

G.name: Devout / Clerical
G.notes:
Calm, measured, reverent. Speaks with quiet authority.
Often invokes faith, fate, or divine will.
Register: 'mayhap', 'blessing', 'grace', 'divine', 'sin', 'fate'.
Avoids slang. Rarely emotional; composed and solemn.
Phrasing often feels sermonic or reflective.

H.name: Menacing / Threatening
H.notes:
Controlled, cold. Power comes from restraint, not shouting.
Short declaratives. Implied violence. Polite on the surface, cruel underneath.
Register: formal archaic for high-status villains; blunt for lower ones.
"I would choose my next words with care." "See that it does not happen again."

I.name: Scholarly / Pedantic
I.notes:
Verbose, precise, enjoys the sound of his own expertise.
Long sentences with qualifications, parentheticals, corrections.
Register: full archaic, technical vocabulary, Latin-flavoured.
"Strictly speaking..." "One must distinguish between..." "As I have noted previously..."
